era_context: Classical Greece is consolidating post-Persian Wars power structures. Athens is rising as a naval hegemon, and the Delian League is either being founded or formally organized around this time. Sparta remains the dominant land power. In China, the Spring and Autumn period is in its final years, with regional fragmentation accelerating. The Achaemenid Persian Empire under Xerxes I continues to dominate western Asia and the eastern Mediterranean.

events.title: Possible founding or formal organization of the Delian League
events.description: The Delian League is traditionally dated to 478 or 477 BCE by ancient sources. This alliance of Greek city-states was formed to continue resistance against Persian aggression and to liberate Greek cities still under Persian control. The league's treasury was initially kept on the island of Delos, hence the name. Athens provided the military leadership and gradually transformed the league into an instrument of Athenian imperial power. The exact founding date is uncertain; Thucydides does not provide a precise year, and later sources vary. By 477 BCE, the league is certainly operational.
key_figures: Aristides the Just (Athenian statesman, associated with league organization)
Pausanias (Spartan general, initially involved in post-Persian Wars operations)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War (1.96–97)

events.certainty_note: Ancient sources give 478 or 477 BCE for the league's founding, but the exact year is uncertain. Thucydides places it after the Persian Wars but does not specify a year. Modern scholarship generally accepts a date within 478–476 BCE. The assignment to 477 BCE is conventional and may be off by 1–2 years.

events.title: Cimon's campaign against Thracian territories and consolidation of Athenian naval dominance
events.description: Cimon leads Athenian forces in campaigns in Thrace and the northern Aegean, extending Athenian influence and control over strategic territories. These campaigns are part of the broader consolidation of Athenian power following the Persian Wars. The capture of Eion and operations against Thracian strongholds strengthen Athenian control of trade routes and establish Athens as the dominant naval power in the region. These victories enhance Cimon's political prestige at Athens.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War (1.98)

events.certainty_note: Cimon's Thracian campaigns are attested in later sources, but precise dating is uncertain. Plutarch and Thucydides do not provide exact years. The campaigns are generally placed in the 470s BCE. Assignment to 477 BCE is conventional.

events.title: Continued fragmentation and warfare in Chinese states during late Spring and Autumn period
events.description: In China, regional states continue to compete for dominance as the Spring and Autumn period approaches its end. Warfare is becoming more frequent and organized. The nominal Zhou dynasty has lost effective control, and powerful feudal lords (ba) pursue their own territorial ambitions. The period is characterized by increasing centralization of power within individual states and the emergence of larger, more organized armies. This era of chaos and competition produces the philosophical schools of Confucianism and Daoism.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of fragmentation and warfare during the late Spring and Autumn period is well-attested in Chinese chronicles. However, specific events for 477 BCE are not clearly distinguished in surviving sources. The year is assigned by convention.

disconfirming_evidence: The Delian League's founding date is disputed. Thucydides does not provide a precise year; later sources vary between 478 and 477 BCE. Some modern scholars argue for 476 BCE or even later. The league's transformation from a defensive alliance to an Athenian empire was gradual, not sudden. Cimon's campaigns are attested in later sources but lack precise dating; some scholars argue that major campaigns traditionally assigned to the 470s BCE may have occurred later, in the 460s BCE. The Spring and Autumn period's end date is a historiographic convention, not a discrete historical event.
historiographic_note: For Greece, sources are secondary and written decades to centuries after events. Thucydides is the most reliable source but does not provide year-by-year precision. Plutarch's biographies, written ~600 years later, are valuable but often blend history with legend and moral instruction. For China, the Shiji is the primary source but was compiled 400 years after the Spring and Autumn period. The Chunqiu is a sparse chronicle, and later commentaries provide interpretations that may not reflect original intent. The dating of specific events in both regions is often uncertain, and the year 477 BCE itself is a modern convention.

graph_edges.note: The Delian League eventually became the Athenian Empire; treasury moved to Athens in 454 BCE
Cimon's campaigns continued to expand Athenian power throughout the 470s BCE
Fragmentation continued until the traditional end of the Spring and Autumn period
